CLARITY.justification: The paper is reasonably well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states main contributions early in the Introduction. Notation for the learning problem and the actor/critic components (ω, at, χ, πθ, Qϕ) is introduced and the algorithm pseudocode is helpful. However the provided text contains many duplicated/repeated paragraphs, LaTeX artifacts and missing/omitted figures and tables that are referenced (Figures 1–6, Tables 1–3), which hinders readability; there are also occasional formatting ambiguities in expressions (e.g. f^t / ∇f t) and minor grammatical issues. These presentation problems make some sections harder to follow despite an otherwise logical flow.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes using an actor-critic (policy + value) RL framework to automatically control SGD learning rates (Sec.1, Sec.3), explicitly optimizing long-term reward (cumulative loss decrease) via a critic and using design choices like feeding different examples to actor and critic to improve generalization (Sec.3.2–3.4). It clearly differentiates from prior adaptive step-size work (e.g. Schaul et al. (2013) and Daniel et al. (2016)) by leveraging learned, long-horizon RL control rather than closed-form or hand-crafted feature methods (Sec.2,4.4). While this is a notable and well-motivated application of existing deep RL techniques to learning-rate scheduling, it is primarily an application/extension of known actor-critic methods rather than a fundamental new theory, hence not rated maximal novelty.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its claims and provides experimental evidence on MNIST and CIFAR-10 against SGD/Adam/Adagrad/RMSprop and vSGD (Sections 4.2–4.4), with learning-rate sweeps for baselines and averages over five runs. The mathematical description follows standard deterministic actor–critic updates (Equations 6–9) but contains minor inconsistencies (actor gradient is taken at the next state and notation is informal) and offers no theoretical convergence analysis. Experimental methodology is reasonable but incomplete: important details (optimizer hyperparameters for actor/critic, stddevs or statistical tests, compute/budget comparisons) are missing, and the RL assumptions (Markovness, stationarity of the environment given changing model parameters) and their impact are not addressed. Overall the contribution is empirically supported but has methodological and theoretical gaps that prevent a higher technical score.
